items.title: Understanding Syncope Incidence and Economic Impact
items.content: Syncope is a prevalent cause of emergency department visits, accounting for approximately 3% of all ED visits. The economic burden is significant, with syncope-related hospitalizations costing around $2.4 billion annually in the United States.
keyPoints: Syncope accounts for ~3% of all emergency department (ED) visits
Annual cost for syncope-related hospitalization in the U.S. is ~$2.4 billion
items.reference: Syncope · Syncope | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.title: Age-Related Incidence and Prognosis of Syncope
items.content: Syncope exhibits a bimodal age distribution, with peak incidence in young individuals (ages 10-30) primarily due to neurally mediated syncope. In older adults, the incidence rises sharply after age 70, often linked to cardiac causes or orthostatic hypotension.
keyPoints: Lifetime cumulative incidence of syncope can be up to 40%
Bimodal age distribution: peak in young (ages 10-30), sharp rise after 70 years
Neurally mediated syncope is common in the young; cardiac causes and orthostatic hypotension are more prevalent in older adults
items.reference: Syncope · Syncope | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.title: Mechanism of Syncope Due to Orthostatic Changes
items.content: Standing upright causes blood pooling in the lower extremities, leading to decreased venous return and cardiac output. This triggers a compensatory reflex via baroreceptors to maintain blood pressure. Failure of this response results in hypotension and cerebral hypoperfusion.
keyPoints: Blood pooling occurs with 500–1000 mL shifting to the lower extremities upon standing.
Compensatory reflex involves increased sympathetic outflow and decreased vagal activity.
Failure of compensatory mechanisms leads to orthostatic hypotension or neurally mediated syncope.

items.title: Cerebral Blood Flow Autoregulation in Syncope
items.content: Syncope is a transient loss of consciousness due to global cerebral hypoperfusion, indicating failure of autoregulatory mechanisms. Cerebral blood flow remains constant over perfusion pressures of 50–150 mmHg but drops below 25 mL/min per 100 g brain tissue results in impaired consciousness.
keyPoints: Cerebral blood flow ranges from 50–60 mL/min per 100 g brain tissue under normal conditions.
Autoregulatory response latency is 5–10 seconds.
Loss of consciousness occurs with cessation of blood flow for 6–8 seconds.

items.title: Mechanism of Neurally Mediated Syncope
items.content: Neurally mediated syncope involves a complex reflex arc that leads to transient changes in autonomic efferent activity, characterized by increased parasympathetic outflow and sympathoinhibition. This results in bradycardia, vasodilation, reduced vasoconstrictor tone (vasodepressor response), and decreased cardiac output, ultimately causing a fall in systemic blood pressure that reduces cerebral blood flow below autoregulatory limits.
keyPoints: Transient change in autonomic efferent activity
Increased parasympathetic outflow and sympathoinhibition
Bradycardia, vasodilation, reduced vasoconstrictor tone (vasodepressor response)
Reduced cardiac output
Fall in systemic blood pressure reduces cerebral blood flow below autoregulatory limits

items.title: Triggers and Classification of Neurally Mediated Syncope
items.content: Neurally mediated syncope can be triggered by various afferent stimuli such as orthostatic stress, carotid sinus stimulation, or visceral organ stimulation. It is classified based on the afferent pathway and provocative trigger into vasovagal syncope (common faint) and situational reflex syncopes. Alternatively, it can be subdivided based on efferent pathways: vasodepressor syncope (sympathetic failure), cardioinhibitory syncope (increased vagal outflow), and mixed response syncope.
keyPoints: Triggers include orthostatic stress, carotid sinus stimulation, visceral organ stimulation
Classification based on afferent pathway: vasovagal syncope, situational reflex syncopes
Subdivisions based on efferent pathways: vasodepressor syncope, cardioinhibitory syncope, mixed response syncope

items.title: Clinical Features and Predisposing Factors of Neurally Mediated Syncope
items.content: Patients with neurally mediated syncope may experience premonitory symptoms such as diaphoresis, pallor, palpitations, nausea, hyperventilation, and yawning. During the syncopal event, myoclonus, upward eye deviation, dilated pupils, roving eye movements, and vocalizations like grunting or moaning can occur. Postictal confusion is rare, but hallucinations and near-death experiences may be reported. Predisposing factors include motionless upright posture, warm temperature, intravascular volume depletion, alcohol ingestion, hypoxemia, anemia, pain, sight of blood, venipuncture, and intense emotion.
keyPoints: Premonitory symptoms: diaphoresis, pallor, palpitations, nausea, hyperventilation, yawning
Syncopal event features: myoclonus, upward eye deviation, dilated pupils, roving eye movements, vocalizations
Postictal confusion rare; hallucinations and near-death experiences possible
Predisposing factors: motionless upright posture, warm temperature, intravascular volume depletion, alcohol ingestion, hypoxemia, anemia, pain, sight of blood, venipuncture, intense emotion

items.title: Management Strategies for Neurally Mediated Syncope
items.content: Reassurance and education are fundamental in managing neurally mediated syncope. Avoidance of triggers, along with plasma volume expansion through increased fluid and salt intake, form the basis of treatment. Isometric counterpressure maneuvers can be effective by raising blood pressure and delaying syncope onset.
keyPoints: Reassurance, education, avoidance of provocative stimuli
Plasma volume expansion with fluid and salt
Isometric counterpressure maneuvers (abdominal and leg muscle tensing most effective)
Randomized controlled trial supports these interventions

items.title: Pharmacologic and Device-Based Interventions for Refractory Neurally Mediated Syncope
items.content: For patients with refractory neurally mediated syncope, fludrocortisone, vasoconstricting agents, and $ \beta $-adrenoreceptor antagonists are used. Midodrine is the only agent proven effective in multicenter trials. Cardiac pacemakers rarely benefit most patients but may help older patients with cardioinhibitory responses on tilt-table testing.
keyPoints: Fludrocortisone, vasoconstricting agents, $ \beta $-adrenoreceptor antagonists used for refractory cases
Midodrine effective in multicenter trials
Cardiac pacemakers rarely beneficial except in older patients (>40 years) with cardioinhibitory response on tilt-table testing

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Orthostatic Hypotension
items.content: Orthostatic hypotension is defined by a specific drop in blood pressure upon standing. It involves a reduction of at least 20 mmHg in systolic or 10 mmHg in diastolic blood pressure after 3 minutes of standing or head-up tilt.
keyPoints: Reduction in systolic BP ≥ 20 mmHg
Reduction in diastolic BP ≥ 10 mmHg
Time frame: After 3 min of standing or head-up tilt

items.title: Symptoms and Variants of Orthostatic Hypotension
items.content: Orthostatic hypotension presents with symptoms like light-headedness, dizziness, and presyncope. It can also manifest as delayed orthostatic hypotension beyond 3 minutes or initial orthostatic hypotension within 15 seconds.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Light-headedness, dizziness, presyncope
Delayed orthostatic hypotension: Occurs > 3 min of standing
Initial orthostatic hypotension: Within 15 s of standing

items.title: Causes and Associated Conditions of Orthostatic Hypotension
items.content: Orthostatic hypotension can result from autonomic nervous system dysfunction, including central and peripheral causes. It is often associated with conditions like multiple system atrophy, Parkinson's disease, and diabetes mellitus.
keyPoints: Causes: Central/peripheral autonomic dysfunction
Associated disorders: Multiple system atrophy, Parkinson's disease, diabetes mellitus
Iatrogenic causes: Medications lowering peripheral resistance

items.title: Management of Orthostatic Hypotension: Nonpharmacologic and Pharmacologic Strategies
items.content: Orthostatic hypotension management begins with removing reversible causes, particularly vasoactive medications. Nonpharmacologic interventions include patient education on staged postural changes, dietary adjustments (increased fluid and salt), isometric counterpressure maneuvers, and raising the bed head to mitigate supine hypertension and nocturnal diuresis. If these measures fail, pharmacologic treatments with fludrocortisone acetate or vasoconstricting agents like midodrine or droxidopa are recommended.
keyPoints: Remove reversible causes such as vasoactive medications.
Nonpharmacologic interventions: staged postural changes, dietary fluid and salt increase, isometric counterpressure maneuvers, raising bed head.
Pharmacologic intervention with fludrocortisone acetate or agents like midodrine or droxidopa if nonpharmacologic measures fail.

items.title: Cardiac Syncope: Arrhythmias and Structural Heart Disease
items.content: Cardiac syncope is primarily caused by arrhythmias or structural heart disease. Bradyarrhythmias, such as those from sinus node dysfunction or AV block, can lead to syncopal episodes. Ventricular tachyarrhythmias also cause syncope, with the likelihood influenced by ventricular rate and function. Genetic mutations in ion channel subunits (e.g., long QT syndrome, Brugada syndrome) contribute to arrhythmogenesis.
keyPoints: Causes: Arrhythmias (bradyarrhythmias, ventricular tachyarrhythmias), structural heart disease.
Bradyarrhythmias: Sinus node dysfunction, AV block.
Ventricular tachyarrhythmias: Likelihood of syncope depends on rate (<200 bpm less likely) and ventricular function.
Genetic disorders: Long QT syndrome (torsades des pointes), Brugada syndrome (SCN5A mutations).

items.title: Structural Heart Disease and Syncope Mechanisms
items.content: Structural heart disease, including valvular disease, myocardial ischemia, cardiomyopathies, cardiac masses (e.g., atrial myxoma), and pericardial effusions, can cause syncope by reducing cardiac output. Additionally, structural diseases may predispose to arrhythmias or lead to orthostatic hypotension through aggressive treatment with diuretics/vasodilators or inappropriate reflex vasodilation in conditions like aortic stenosis.
keyPoints: Structural heart disease causes syncope by reducing cardiac output.
Examples: Valvular disease, myocardial ischemia, cardiomyopathies, atrial myxoma, pericardial effusions.
Mechanisms: Arrhythmogenesis predisposition, orthostatic hypotension from diuretics/vasodilators, reflex vasodilation in aortic stenosis and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.title: Differentiating Syncope from Seizures
items.content: Syncope can be confused with seizures, but distinguishing features exist. Myoclonic jerks in syncope are arrhythmic and short (<30 s), while postictal drowsiness lasts >5 min after a seizure.
keyPoints: Myoclonic movements occur in up to 90% of syncopal episodes.
Syncope myoclonus is typically arrhythmic and <30 seconds.
Postictal drowsiness from seizures lasts >5 minutes.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.title: Identifying Hypoglycemia as a Cause of Transient Loss of Consciousness
items.content: Hypoglycemia, especially in insulin-treated diabetics, can mimic syncope. Symptoms include tremor, palpitations, anxiety, diaphoresis, hunger, and paresthesias due to autonomic activation.
keyPoints: Hunger is a key symptom of hypoglycemia but not typical for syncope.
Repeated hypoglycemia can impair the counterregulatory response.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.title: Distinguishing Vasovagal Syncope from Psychiatric Disorders
items.content: Vasovagal syncope is characterized by hypotension and sometimes bradycardia, unlike psychiatric disorders which lack significant hemodynamic changes.
keyPoints: Vasovagal syncope involves hypotension and possibly bradycardia.
Psychiatric disorders causing apparent loss of consciousness show no significant hemodynamic changes.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.title: Identifying High-Risk Features for Syncope
items.content: The initial evaluation of a patient presenting with transient loss of consciousness aims to determine if the episode was syncope, identify its cause, and assess risk for future episodes or serious harm. Key components include detailed history-taking, eyewitness accounts, physical and neurologic exams, and specific tests like blood pressure measurements in different positions.
keyPoints: High-risk features on history include new onset of chest discomfort, abdominal pain, shortness of breath, headache; syncope during exertion or while supine; sudden palpitations followed by syncope; severe coronary artery or structural heart disease.
Examination high-risk features: systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg, gastrointestinal hemorrhage signs, persistent bradycardia (<40 beats/min), undiagnosed systolic murmur.

ECG is crucial if arrhythmia or cardiac disease is suspected; abnormalities include bradyarrhythmias, tachyarrhythmias, AV block, acute myocardial ischemia, old myocardial infarction, long QT, bundle branch block.

items.title: Appropriate Use of Cardiac Evaluation in Syncope
items.content: Cardiac evaluation is essential for patients with syncope when there's a high pretest probability of arrhythmia. This includes ECG monitoring, Holter monitoring for frequent syncopal episodes, and loop recorders for less frequent episodes. Treadmill exercise testing can identify exercise-induced arrhythmias or vasodilation.
keyPoints: ECG monitoring is indicated for patients with high pretest probability of arrhythmia causing syncope.
Continuous ambulatory ECG (Holter) monitoring recommended for daily syncopal episodes; loop recorders for suspected arrhythmias with low risk of sudden cardiac death.
Treadmill exercise testing with ECG and blood pressure monitoring is useful for syncope during or after exercise.

items.title: Role of Autonomic Nervous System Testing in Syncope Evaluation
items.content: Autonomic nervous system testing, including tilt-table tests, can uncover autonomic failure and predisposition to neurally mediated syncope. These tests assess various functions of the autonomic nervous system and help distinguish between orthostatic hypotension due to autonomic failure and neurally mediated syncope.
keyPoints: Autonomic testing includes assessments like heart rate variability, thermoregulatory sweat response, and blood pressure response to Valsalva maneuver.
Tilt-table test helps identify patients with immediate or delayed orthostatic hypotension.

items.question: What is the lifetime cumulative incidence of syncope in the general population?
items.explanation: The passage states that syncope has a lifetime cumulative incidence of up to 40% in the general population.
items.reference: Syncope · Syncope | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.question: In which age group is neurally mediated syncope most commonly observed?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the peak incidence of neurally mediated syncope in young individuals occurs between ages 10 and 30.
items.reference: Syncope · Syncope | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.question: Which cause of syncope is associated with an increased risk of sudden cardiac death?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that syncope due to a cardiac cause is associated with an increased risk of sudden cardiac death.
items.reference: Syncope · Syncope | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.question: Which physiological response is initiated by baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch during standing to compensate for decreased venous return?
options: Decreased sympathetic outflow and increased vagal nerve activity
Increased sympathetic outflow and decreased vagal nerve activity
Increased parasympathetic outflow and decreased sympathetic nerve activity
No change in autonomic nervous system activity
items.explanation: The compensatory reflex response to standing involves increased sympathetic outflow and decreased vagal nerve activity.

items.question: What is the typical range of cerebral blood flow per 100 g brain tissue under normal conditions?
items.explanation: Cerebral blood flow typically ranges from 50–60 mL/min per 100 g brain tissue.

items.question: What EEG pattern is characterized by a sudden flattening of the EEG, indicating more severe cerebral hypoperfusion?
items.explanation: The 'slow-flat-slow' EEG pattern includes sudden flattening, marking more severe cerebral hypoperfusion.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a premonitory feature of autonomic activation in neurally mediated syncope?
items.explanation: Premonitory features include diaphoresis, pallor, nausea, but not bradycardia.

items.question: What is the primary efferent response mechanism in neurally mediated syncope?
items.explanation: The primary efferent response involves increased parasympathetic outflow and sympathoinhibition.

items.question: Which of the following is a trigger for vasovagal syncope?
items.explanation: Vasovagal syncope can be triggered by orthostatic stress.

items.question: Which of the following is a rare feature during a syncopal event in neurally mediated syncope?
items.explanation: Postictal confusion is rare during a syncopal event.

items.question: Which maneuver is considered the most effective in raising blood pressure during neurally mediated syncope?
options: Tensing of the abdominal muscles only
items.explanation: Abdominal and leg muscle tensing is noted as the most effective maneuver for raising blood pressure.

items.question: Which medication has been shown to be effective in international, multicenter randomized controlled trials for treating refractory neurally mediated syncope?
items.explanation: Only midodrine has been shown to be effective in international, multicenter randomized controlled trials.

items.question: In which patient population might a dual-chamber pacemaker with a closed-loop stimulation algorithm decrease syncope recurrence?
options: Patients under 40 years of age with vasovagal syncope
Older patients (>40 years) with frequent recurrence and cardioinhibitory response on tilt-table test
All patients with neurally mediated syncope
Young adults with a history of orthostatic hypotension
items.explanation: Dual-chamber pacing may be helpful in older patients (>40 years) with frequent recurrence and a cardioinhibitory response on tilt-table test.

items.question: What is the minimum reduction in systolic blood pressure that defines orthostatic hypotension?
items.explanation: Orthostatic hypotension is defined by a reduction in systolic blood pressure of at least 20 mmHg.

items.question: Which symptom is NOT typically associated with orthostatic hypotension?
items.explanation: Orthostatic hypotension symptoms include light-headedness, dizziness, and presyncope; acute chest pain is not typical.

items.question: What condition may be present in more than 50% of patients with orthostatic hypotension due to autonomic failure?
items.explanation: Supine hypertension is common in patients with orthostatic hypotension due to autonomic failure.

items.question: Which of the following disorders is NOT classified as a synucleinopathy?
items.explanation: Diabetic neuropathy is not a synucleinopathy; the others are classified as such due to $\alpha$-synuclein aggregation.

items.question: Which of the following is a recommended nonpharmacologic intervention for orthostatic hypotension?
options: Patient education regarding staged moves from supine to upright
Reducing dietary fluid and salt intake
items.explanation: Staged moves from supine to upright are recommended as a nonpharmacologic intervention for orthostatic hypotension.

items.question: Which medication is NOT typically used in pharmacologic intervention for orthostatic hypotension?
items.explanation: Lisinopril is not typically used in the pharmacologic intervention for orthostatic hypotension.

items.question: What can cause syncope due to bradyarhythmias?
items.explanation: Severe sinus node dysfunction is a cause of syncope due to bradyarhythmias.

items.question: Which condition is associated with exercise- or stress-induced ventricular arrhythmias?
items.explanation: Catecholaminergic polymorphic tachycardia is associated with exercise- or stress-induced ventricular arrhythmias.

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.question: Which of the following features is typically associated with syncope rather than seizures?
options: Muscle aches lasting longer after the event
Reorientation occurring almost immediately after the event
Loss of consciousness usually lasts more than 5 minutes
items.explanation: Reorientation occurs almost immediately after syncope, whereas it is prolonged after seizures.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.question: Which premonitory feature is more characteristic of hypoglycemia than syncope?
items.explanation: Hunger is a premonitory feature of hypoglycemia, not typically associated with syncope.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.question: Which condition involves maintaining consciousness during an episode?
items.explanation: In cataplexy, consciousness is maintained despite the loss of muscular tone.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.question: What differentiates autonomic seizures from syncope in terms of cardiovascular manifestations?
options: Autonomic seizures do not involve cardiovascular changes.
Syncope involves significant tachycardias and bradycardias.
Autonomic seizures include clinically significant tachycardias and bradycardias.
Both conditions have identical cardiovascular manifestations.
items.explanation: Autonomic seizures can cause significant tachycardias or bradycardias, unlike typical syncope.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a high-risk feature on history for syncope?
options: Syncope during exertion or while supine
Sudden onset of palpitations followed by syncope
items.explanation: Persistent bradycardia is a high-risk feature on examination, not history.

items.question: Which test should be avoided in patients with carotid bruits or known stenosis?
items.explanation: Carotid sinus massage should be avoided in patients with carotid bruits or known stenosis.

items.question: In which scenario is continuous ambulatory electrocardiographic monitoring recommended?
options: For patients with infrequent syncopal episodes
For patients experiencing frequent syncopal episodes (e.g., daily or almost daily)
Only for patients with a family history of sudden cardiac death
When there are no suspected arrhythmias
items.explanation: Continuous ambulatory electrocardiographic monitoring is recommended for patients who experience frequent syncopal episodes.

items.question: Which of the following is a reason to perform echocardiography in syncope evaluation?
options: Abnormalities found on physical examination or ECG
items.explanation: Echocardiography is performed if abnormalities are found on physical examination or the ECG.

items.statement: Orthostatic hypotension is more common in community-dwelling elderly individuals than in institutionalized ones.
items.explanation: The passage states that orthostatic hypotension is more common in institutionalized than community-dwelling elderly individuals.
items.reference: Syncope · Syncope | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.statement: Neurally mediated syncope has a poor prognosis with affected life expectancy.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that syncope of noncardiac and unexplained origin in younger individuals, which includes neurally mediated syncope, has an excellent prognosis.
items.reference: Syncope · Syncope | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.statement: A fall in systemic systolic blood pressure to approximately 50 mmHg or lower usually results in syncope.
items.explanation: Syncope typically occurs when systemic systolic blood pressure falls to around 50 mmHg or lower.

items.statement: EEG seizure discharges are commonly detected during syncopal events.
items.explanation: Despite motor activity, EEG seizure discharges are not detected in syncopal events.

items.statement: Fecal incontinence is common during neurally mediated syncope.
items.explanation: Fecal incontinence is very rare during neurally mediated syncope.

items.statement: A functioning autonomic nervous system is necessary for neurally mediated syncope.
items.explanation: Neurally mediated syncope requires a functioning autonomic nervous system.

items.statement: A cardiac pacemaker is rarely beneficial for most patients with neurally mediated syncope.
items.explanation: Use of a cardiac pacemaker is rarely beneficial due to the dominant pathophysiologic mechanisms being vasodilation, decreased central blood volume, and decreased stroke volume.

items.statement: Isometric counterpressure maneuvers are ineffective in patients with a long prodrome.
items.explanation: These maneuvers may be particularly helpful in patients with a long prodrome by avoiding or delaying the onset of syncope.

items.statement: Orthostatic hypotension can occur within 15 seconds of standing and resolve within 45 seconds.
items.explanation: This describes initial orthostatic hypotension, a transient mismatch between cardiac output and peripheral vascular resistance.

items.statement: Orthostatic hypotension is always associated with autonomic failure.
items.explanation: While often due to autonomic failure, orthostatic hypotension can also be iatrogenic or related to other conditions.

items.statement: Ventricular tachyarrhythmias are more likely to cause syncope in patients with good ventricular function.
items.explanation: Patients with poor ventricular function are more likely to have syncope at a given ventricular rate.

items.statement: Structural heart disease can lead to syncope by compromising cardiac output.
items.explanation: Structural heart disease may compromise cardiac output, leading to syncope.

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.statement: Fecal incontinence is a common feature of syncope.
items.explanation: Fecal incontinence occurs very rarely with syncope.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.statement: Seizures are often provoked by emotions or pain, unlike syncope.
items.explanation: Seizures are rarely provoked by emotions or pain; this is more typical of syncope.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.statement: Baseline laboratory blood tests are often helpful in identifying the cause of syncope.
items.explanation: Baseline laboratory blood tests are rarely helpful unless specific disorders are suspected.

items.statement: Tilt-table testing can help distinguish between orthostatic hypotension due to autonomic failure and neurally mediated syncope.
items.explanation: The tilt-table test helps identify hemodynamic abnormalities that differentiate these conditions.

items.assertion: Hyperventilation plays a central role in situational reflex syncopes.
items.reason: It leads to hypocarbia and cerebral vasoconstriction, impairing venous return to the heart.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The underlying basis for different syncope thresholds among individuals is well understood.
items.reason: Genetic factors have been consistently identified across large cohorts.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the underlying basis is not well known, and genetic factors are inconsistent.

items.assertion: Reassurance and education are part of managing neurally mediated syncope.
items.reason: These interventions help patients understand their condition and avoid triggers, which is crucial in management.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why reassurance and education are important.

items.assertion: Supine hypertension is a common finding in patients with orthostatic hypotension.
items.reason: This can be explained by baroreflex dysfunction and residual sympathetic outflow, particularly in central autonomic degeneration.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.reason: Drugs that lower peripheral resistance can cause orthostatic hypotension.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The long QT syndrome is associated with a predisposition to ventricular arrhythmias.
items.reason: It involves prolonged cardiac repolarization due to mutations in ion channel subunit genes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Brugada syndrome is characterized by structural heart disease.
items.reason: It involves idiopathic ventricular fibrillation with right ventricular ECG abnormalities.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Brugada syndrome does not involve structural heart disease.

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.assertion: Patients with cataplexy experience loss of consciousness.
items.reason: Cataplexy involves a partial or complete loss of muscular tone triggered by emotions.
items.explanation: In cataplexy, consciousness is maintained despite the loss of muscle tone.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.assertion: Hypoglycemia can cause transient loss of consciousness.
items.reason: The clinical features associated with hypoglycemia include tremor, palpitations, and anxiety due to autonomic activation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why hypoglycemia can cause transient loss of consciousness.
items.reference: Syncope · Cardiac Syncope | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.assertion: ECG monitoring is indicated for patients with a high pretest probability of arrhythmia causing syncope.
items.reason: Patients with severe coronary artery or structural heart disease often have a higher risk of life-threatening arrhythmias.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ECG monitoring is indicated.

items.assertion: Invasive electrophysiologic studies should be performed routinely in all patients with syncope.
items.reason: These tests have high sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing arrhythmias.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because invasive electrophysiologic studies are rarely performed unless a high pretest probability exists; the reason is also false as these tests have low sensitivity and specificity.
